text:
A new theory has emerged which appears to explain why the swimming at the Paris 2024 Olympic Games has been "embarrassingly slow".

Swimming experts have put forward a wild conspiracy theory as to why no world records had been broken at the Paris 2024 Olympic Games in the pool.

As the Games entered the third day of competition, broadcasters and online commentators have pointed out the depth of the pools was likely to blame.

Australia’s own Ariarne Titmus, who recently won gold in the “race of the century” 400m freestyle, had actually fallen short by two seconds of her own world record.

Both Titmus and Mollie O’Callaghan fell short of their own personal bests in the 200m freestyle final, despite O’Callaghan breaking an Olympic record and winning the gold medal over her fellow Aussie.

Additionally, the 4x100m freestyle relay team broke their Olympic record, but came up short to improving on their world record set at last year’s World Aquatic Championships.

One social media user posted on X asking the “nugget from the pundits” who described the pool as “slow”.

“Can someone explain why the pool at the Olympics is ‘slow’? Is the water the wrong viscosity? Or maybe there’s a headwind? Slightly uphill?” they wrote.

Another user said it was “actually fairly straightforward. The pool is too shallow.”

“More shallow = more chop = slow pool,” the X user wrote.

According to swimming news organisation SwimSwam, pool water depth might be the “biggest factor” in making a pool “fast”.

“Scientifically speaking, the deeper the water depth, the faster the pool. In a shallow pool, waves will “bounce” or reflect off the bottom of the pool, which causes the entire pool to become more turbulent or ‘wavy,’” according to a2016 SwimSwam article.

Editor in Chief of SwimSwam Braden Keith jokingly said the “impact of the slow pools” was the publication's workload covering national records had been “dramatically reduced”.

Following Romania’s David Popovici’s win in the 200m freestyle on Tuesday, Mr Keith said he would not have won the gold medal at any Olympic Games since 2000 as Australia’s own Ian Thorpe was 0.1 faster in 2004.

“A win's a win though, the pool is most definitely slow,” he wrote.

While the pool at Paris La Défense Arena is 2.15 metres deep, World Aquatics recommended a minimum of 2.5 metres for the Games.

The shallow depth has been said to be affecting breaststroke races in particular with the winner of the men’s 100m breaststroke event, Italy’s Nicolò Martinenghi, clocked in at 59.02sec, the slowest time since the Athens 2004 Olympic Games.

In comparison, Britain’s Adam Peaty set the world record at 56.88sec and won the gold medal at the last Games in Tokyo with a time of 57.37sec. The Olympic pool in Japan was three metres deep.

Slow pool allegations are real.That 100 breast was embarrassingly slow

Former competitive swimmer and current 100m freestyle world record holder Cate Campbell told Channel 9 viewers the pool was “slightly shallower than we would normally see”, adding swimmers would “normally want a three-metre pool”.

After her 400m freestyle win, Titmus said it was about “getting a hand on the wall first”, not about swimming “as fast as you can” to set a new world record.

Host country swimmer, France’s Leon Marchand, as well as Canada’s Summer McIntosh, both set new world records in the 400m medley in the past 12 months but couldn’t match their previous performance in Paris.

Despite this, Marchand’s coach Bob Bowman, the former coach of the most decorated Olympian of all time, Michael Phelps, said his swimmers had observed the pool was on the shallow side.

“They’ve noticed that it’s shallower, which it is, because particularly a lot of my swimmers are underwater. So it looks different because they’re closer to the bottom,” Bowman said.

Bowman added a swimmer would have to be “very outstanding” to break a world record in Paris.

“It’ll have to be a significant swim,” he said.
